frontmatter.title: Push a package to the Cloud

internal.content:
--- title: Push a package to the Cloud --- import { CarbonForIbmDotcom } from "@carbon/pictograms-react"; import { ArtTools_01 } from "@carbon/pictograms-react"; import { FileBackup } from "@carbon/pictograms-react"; import { Tools } from "@carbon/pictograms-react"; The `elaunch.py` command line tool executes and monitors virtual experiments. You can use it to run experiments on your laptop or on a High Performance Computing Cluster. When you submit a virtual experiment via ST4SD API it is executed by `elaunch.py`. ## Install elaunch.py If you haven't already, install the `st4sd-runtime-core` python package: ``` pip install "st4sd-runtime-core[develop]>=2.5.1" ``` ## Running experiments with elaunch.py Use elaunch.py to run virtual experiments: `elaunch.py `. Note, running experiments locally requires using either Linux or MacOS. Windows users should either use a Virtual Machine (e.g. [VirtualBox](https://www.virtualbox.org) etc) or the Windows Subsystem for Linux ([WSL](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install)). Some experiments, like the one in this example, also require you to install one of [docker](https://docs.docker.com/engine/install/), [podman](https://podman.io/docs/installation), or [Rancher Desktop](https://docs.rancherdesktop.io/getting-started/installation/). In this example you will run the workflow [`nanopore-geometry-experiment`](https://registry.st4sd.res.ibm.com/registry-ui/experiment/nanopore-geometry-experiment) using docker. It consists of the following computational graph: ![The graph of nanopore-geometry-experiment](../../assets/images/examples/nanopore-geometry-experiment.png) To execute it first download its code using `git`: ```bash git clone https://github.com/st4sd/nanopore-geometry-experiment.git cd nanopore-geometry-experiment ``` Then run elaunch.py specifying certain files in the directory created above: ```bash elaunch.py -i docker-example/cif_files.dat -l 40 --nostamp\ --applicationDependencySource="nanopore-database=cif:copy" \ nanopore-geometry-experiment.package ``` The experiment should take about 5 minutes to complete. The `-l 40` option keeps the log printouts to the bare minimum so don't worry if the command is silent for a few minutes. When the experiment completes expect to see something similar to the following text on your terminal: ``` completed-on=2024-03-15 14:39:29.909105 cost=0 created-on=2024-03-15 14:38:04.402969 current-stage=stage3 exit-status=Success experiment-state=finished stage-progress=1.0 stage-state=finished stages=['stage0', 'stage1', 'stage2', 'stage3'] total-progress=1.0 updated=2024-03-15 14:39:33.804727 updated-on=2024-03-15 14:39:33.804727 ``` If the experiment works `elaunch.py` prints `exit-status=Success` before it terminates and then exits with return code 0. You can find more information about the status of your experiment under the file `${package_name}-${timestamp}.instance/output/status.txt`. For example, here's a `status.txt` for a successful run of an experiment: ``` completed-on=2025-03-21 13:15:19.790371 cost=0 created-on=2025-03-21 13:14:30.088069 current-stage=stage2 exit-status=Success experiment-state=finished stage-progress=1.0 stage-state=finished stages=['stage0', 'stage1', 'stage2'] total-progress=1.0 updated=2025-03-21 13:15:19.799143 updated-on=2025-03-21 13:15:19.799143 ``` ## What is the output of my experiment ? Looking inside the instance directory created for the experiment, we see the following: ``` ls -lth nanopore-geometry-experiment.instance total 64 drwxrwxr-x@ 10 user wheel 320B 21 Mar 13:15 output/ -rw-r--r--@ 1 user wheel 28K 21 Mar 13:15 status.db -rw-rw-r--@ 1 user wheel 536B 21 Mar 13:14 elaunch.yaml drwxr-xr-x@ 5 user wheel 160B 21 Mar 13:14 hooks/ drwxrwxr-x@ 5 user wheel 160B 21 Mar 13:14 stages/ drwxr-xr-x@ 5 user wheel 160B 21 Mar 13:14 conf/ lrwxrwxr-x@ 1 user wheel 31B 21 Mar 13:14 python@ -> /Users/user/venvs/st4sd drwxrwxr-x@ 3 user wheel 96B 21 Mar 13:14 input/ drwxr-xr-x@ 4 user wheel 128B 21 Mar 13:03 nanopore-database/ ``` #### Where can I see what steps were executed during the experiment? The **`stages`** directory contains the working directories for each step executed during this experiment instance. A *stage* represents a logical grouping of components within a workflow. Most experiments include a single stage named `stage0`, but in this case, there are three: `stage0`, `stage1`, and `stage2`. Each working directory includes the files generated by a component, along with its standard output and error logs. Examining these directories is helpful for debugging or understanding how each component behaves. For example, take a look inside `stages/stage1/Zeo0` to see the files produced by the `Zeo` component. The trailing `0` indicates that only one instance of this component was run. We'll cover component replication and stages in different tutorials, so you don't need to worry about those concepts just yet. #### Where can I find the properties that my experiment measured ? The **`output`** directory contains the measured properties of the input systems that were processed, along with metadata about the key outputs of your experiment. Some experiments define an `interface` that specifies which properties are measured for each system. The `nanopore-geometry-experiment` is one such example. You can find the measured properties in the file `${instance-directory}/output/properties.csv`. Here is an example of the output generated by `nanopore-geometry-experiment`: ``` input-id;d_is;d_fs;d_isfs;asa_m^2/cm^3;asa_m^2/g;nasa_m^2/cm^3;nasa_m^2/g;unitcell_volume;density;av_volume_fraction;av_cm^3/g;nav_volume_fraction;nav_cm^3/g;n_pockets CoRE2019/GUJVOX_clean;3.98106;2.74095;3.93078;0.0;0.0;760.654;564.703;1164.59;1.347;0.0;0.0;0.016015;0.0118894;1.0 ``` The CSV file always includes an `input-id` column, which identifies the input systems for which properties were measured. The remaining columns list the specific properties measured for each system. ## Learn more
}> See our tutorial on using [elaunch.py CLI](/direct-run) for more information about running experiments locally.
